content.text: Analytics, ML & Other AWS Service Categories
Beyond compute, storage, databases, and networking, AWS provides four more service pillars:
for operating infrastructure. Together these services complete the platform needed to build modern data-driven applications without managing undifferentiated infrastructure.

attrs.alt: AWS analytics, machine learning, app integration, and developer tool service categories

content.text: AWS analytics services span the full spectrum of data workloads, from real-time stream processing to batch ETL to ad-hoc SQL queries over data lakes.
Kinesis is a family of services for ingesting and processing streaming data in real time — IoT telemetry, clickstream events, application logs, financial transactions.
Low-latency (<70ms) data stream. Producers write records into shards; consumers (Lambda, KCL applications) read and process them. Retention 1–365 days. Each shard handles 1 MB/s write, 2 MB/s read.
Serverless delivery stream. No consumer code needed — Firehose automatically buffers records and delivers to S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, or Splunk. Supports in-flight transformation via Lambda.
Run Apache Flink applications on a managed cluster for stateful stream processing — windowed aggregations, complex event patterns, streaming joins.
Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka. Full Kafka API compatibility for teams already using Kafka. MSK Serverless auto-scales throughput without capacity planning.

import boto3, json, time

kinesis = boto3.client("kinesis", region_name="us-east-1")

# Put a record into Kinesis Data Streams
kinesis.put_record(
    StreamName="clickstream",
    Data=json.dumps({"userId": "u-123", "event": "page_view", "ts": time.time()}),
    PartitionKey="u-123"  # determines shard routing
)
Glue is a fully managed ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service. It has two main components: the
(a Hive-compatible metadata store for schemas across S3, RDS, Redshift, and other sources) and
(PySpark or Python shell scripts that run on managed Spark clusters).
Automatically discover schema from S3, RDS, or Redshift and populate the Data Catalog. Schedule crawlers to run when new partitions are added.
Write PySpark using the GlueContext API (DynamicFrame instead of DataFrame) for automatic schema handling. Jobs run on managed Spark clusters scaled to your DPU (Data Processing Unit) count.
Visual data preparation — profile datasets, apply 250+ built-in transforms, and detect data quality issues without writing code. Useful for data analysts who aren't engineers.
Define DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language) rules to validate data freshness, completeness, and referential integrity before it lands in your warehouse or data lake.
Amazon Athena — Serverless SQL on S3
Athena runs standard SQL queries directly on data in S3 using Presto/Trino under the hood. There is no infrastructure to provision — you pay only for data scanned ($5 per TB).
Partition data by date/region/category in S3 and use columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) to reduce the data scanned per query by 10–100×. Compression (Snappy, GZIP) further reduces cost.
Athena can query DynamoDB, RDS, CloudWatch, DocumentDB, and many more sources via Lambda-based connectors, joining across heterogeneous systems in a single SQL statement.
Submit PySpark workloads to Athena notebooks for interactive data exploration without provisioning an EMR cluster.

-- Partition pruning: query only Jan 2024 data
SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS events
FROM clickstream
WHERE year = '2024' AND month = '01'  -- partition columns
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY events DESC
LIMIT 100;

-- Create external table over Parquet in S3
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE clickstream (
  user_id STRING, event STRING, ts DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year STRING, month STRING)
STORED AS PARQUET
LOCATION 's3://my-data-lake/clickstream/';

AWS ML services range from fully managed foundation models (Bedrock) to custom model training infrastructure (SageMaker) to specialized pre-built AI APIs (Rekognition, Comprehend, Polly, Transcribe) that require no ML expertise to use.

SageMaker provides a unified environment for every stage of the ML lifecycle: data labeling, feature engineering, model training, hyperparameter tuning, model evaluation, deployment, and monitoring in production.
Web-based IDE for notebooks, pipelines, experiments, and model registry — all in one interface.
Spin up a managed cluster (any EC2 instance type, including GPU families like p4d and g5), run your training script (PyTorch, TensorFlow, Scikit-learn, XGBoost), and the cluster terminates when done.
Define CI/CD pipelines for ML — data processing → training → evaluation → conditional model registration → deployment — with full lineage tracking.
Deploy models to SageMaker Endpoints backed by auto-scaled EC2 instances. Multi-model endpoints co-host thousands of models on one endpoint to reduce costs for low-traffic models.
For infrequent traffic, serverless endpoints scale to zero and charge per invocation + GB-second — no idle capacity cost.
Amazon Bedrock — Foundation Models as a Service

Bedrock provides access to foundation models (FMs) from Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Amazon, Cohere, and Stability AI via a single API. You don't manage model infrastructure — call the API and pay per token.
Call InvokeModel or Converse API with your prompt. Different models have different context windows, pricing, and capability profiles (reasoning, coding, image generation, embeddings).

Build retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines by connecting Bedrock to a vector store (OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, Pinecone). Bedrock handles chunking, embedding, and retrieval automatically.
Orchestrate multi-step workflows where the model decides which tools to call (Lambda functions, API schemas, knowledge bases) to fulfill a user request — the model handles the reasoning loop.

Fine-tune Amazon Titan or Llama models on your own labeled dataset within Bedrock (data never leaves your VPC). Use continued pre-training for domain adaptation or instruction fine-tuning for task specialization.

import boto3, json

bedrock = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name="us-east-1")

response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6",
    body=json.dumps({
        "anthropic_version": "bedrock-2023-05-31",
        "max_tokens": 1024,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain VPC in one paragraph."}]
    })
)
result = json.loads(response["body"].read())
print(result["content"][0]["text"])
Object detection, facial recognition, content moderation
Application integration services decouple components so they can scale and fail independently. The four core services each serve a distinct pattern.
SQS provides fully managed message queues for decoupling producers and consumers. A producer writes a message; one consumer reads and processes it, then deletes it.
At-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering, effectively unlimited throughput. Suitable for most workloads.
Exactly-once processing, strict first-in-first-out order within a message group. Max 3,000 TPS with batching (300 TPS without).
After a consumer receives a message, it is hidden for the visibility timeout period (default 30s). If not deleted within that window, it becomes visible again for another consumer to retry.
After maxReceiveCount failed processing attempts, messages are moved to a DLQ. Inspect DLQ messages to diagnose poison-pill failures without blocking the main queue.

import boto3

sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
url = "https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/orders"

# Producer: send a message
sqs.send_message(QueueUrl=url, MessageBody='{"orderId": "ord-123", "amount": 59.99}')

# Consumer: poll and process
msgs = sqs.receive_message(QueueUrl=url, MaxNumberOfMessages=10, WaitTimeSeconds=20)
for msg in msgs.get("Messages", []):
    process(msg["Body"])
    sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=url, ReceiptHandle=msg["ReceiptHandle"])

SNS fans out a single message to multiple subscribers simultaneously. A publisher writes to a topic; SNS pushes copies to all subscribed endpoints: SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints, email, or SMS.
SNS topic → multiple SQS queues allows one event to trigger independent downstream workflows in parallel without coupling the producer to consumers.
Subscribers can define filter policies (JSON attribute matching) so they only receive the subset of messages relevant to them, avoiding unnecessary Lambda invocations.
Combined with SQS FIFO, maintains strict ordering and deduplication across a fan-out topology.
EventBridge is a serverless event bus that routes events from AWS services, custom applications, and 200+ SaaS integrations (Salesforce, Zendesk, GitHub) to targets based on content-based rules.
Default bus (AWS service events), custom buses (your application events), and partner event buses (SaaS sources). Cross-account delivery is supported.
JSON event pattern matching filters events by source, detail-type, or any field in the event body. A rule with no pattern passes all events. A rule can route to multiple targets.

EventBridge Pipes connect a source (SQS, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis) directly to a target with optional filtering and enrichment (Lambda) — replacing common Lambda-passthrough patterns with zero-code wiring.

EventBridge Scheduler replaces CloudWatch Events Scheduled Rules with a standalone service supporting one-time and recurring schedules, rate expressions, and cron expressions at per-minute granularity.

Step Functions orchestrates multi-step processes as state machines defined in Amazon States Language (ASL). Each state can invoke Lambda, ECS tasks, SageMaker jobs, DynamoDB, SNS, SQS, or any AWS SDK integration directly — no glue Lambda needed.
Exactly-once execution, up to 1 year duration, full execution history. Ideal for long-running business processes — order fulfillment, document approval, ML pipelines.
At-least-once execution, up to 5 minutes, much lower cost ($1 per million state transitions vs $0.025 per Standard state transition). For high-throughput event processing.
Built-in Retry (exponential backoff, max attempts) and Catch (route to fallback state on specific error types) — no try/catch boilerplate in Lambda functions.
Iterate over an array of items in parallel with configurable concurrency, replacing the anti-pattern of spawning many Lambda functions for parallel work.
AWS provides a full suite of tools for infrastructure-as-code, observability, systems management, and CI/CD pipelines.

IaC in JSON or YAML. Stacks manage resource lifecycles — create, update, delete together. StackSets deploy the same template across multiple accounts and regions. Drift detection identifies manual changes.

Define infrastructure using TypeScript, Python, Java, or Go. CDK synthesizes to CloudFormation templates. Constructs are reusable patterns — L1 (raw CFN), L2 (opinionated, batteries-included), L3 (patterns like a complete VPC with load balancer).

Metrics, logs, dashboards, and alarms for all AWS services and custom applications. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries log data with a SQL-like syntax. Contributor Insights identifies top-N contributors to traffic or errors.
Manage EC2 instances without SSH keys: Session Manager for shell access, Patch Manager for OS patching, Run Command for remote execution, Parameter Store for configuration. No bastion host needed.

Native CI/CD services. CodePipeline orchestrates source → build → test → deploy stages. CodeBuild runs builds in ephemeral Docker containers. CodeDeploy automates blue/green and rolling deployments to EC2, ECS, or Lambda.
Any service → N consumers via rules

EMR (Elastic MapReduce) runs Apache Spark, Hive, Flink, Presto, and other open-source big-data frameworks on auto-scaled EC2 clusters. Unlike Glue's opinionated ETL jobs, EMR gives you full control over the cluster configuration, framework versions, and Spark tuning parameters.
Transient clusters spin up, run a job, and terminate — ideal for batch workloads. Persistent clusters stay running for interactive analysis with EMR Studio notebooks.
Spot Instances can reduce EMR cluster costs by 60–80%. EMR Instance Fleets mix instance types to maximize spot availability and automatically replace interrupted nodes.
Submit Spark or Hive jobs without managing a cluster. EMR Serverless provisions workers on demand, scales to zero when idle, and charges per vCPU-hour and GB-hour of memory used.
Submit Spark jobs to a shared EKS cluster via EMR virtual clusters. Container isolation allows multiple teams to share the same Kubernetes cluster with independent job queues.

QuickSight is a fully managed cloud BI service. Authors build dashboards with drag-and-drop or SPICE (Super-fast Parallel In-memory Calculation Engine) to pre-aggregate data for sub-second query performance. Readers view dashboards without needing AWS accounts — they authenticate via email or embedded iframes.
An in-memory dataset imported from your data source. Queries run against SPICE rather than hitting Redshift or Athena directly, dramatically reducing query cost and improving dashboard load times.
QuickSight dashboards embed in SaaS products via anonymous embedding or 1-click URL signing. Per-session pricing ($0.30/session) avoids paying for idle readers.
QuickSight Q lets business users ask questions in plain English ("What were sales by region last quarter?") and generates visualizations automatically using ML.
If a partition key is skewed (all events use the same key), one shard receives all traffic and throttles. Use a high-cardinality key (user ID, order ID) or append a random suffix to distribute load.
Without job bookmarks enabled, a Glue ETL job re-processes the entire S3 prefix on every run. Enable bookmarks to track which objects were already processed.
State machines pass state between steps as JSON in the execution context (max 256 KB). For large payloads (file contents, model outputs), write to S3 and pass the S3 key between states instead.
Each event bus supports up to 300 rules. If you have many fine-grained rules, consider routing through a Lambda that performs custom routing logic to avoid hitting the limit.

For time-partitioned data, enable Partition Projection so Athena calculates partition paths from a formula rather than querying the Glue Data Catalog for every query — eliminates metadata bottlenecks at high query rates.
Amazon API Gateway — Managed API Endpoint

API Gateway creates, publishes, and manages REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs at any scale. It acts as the front door for backend services including Lambda, EC2, ECS, and any HTTP endpoint — handling authentication, throttling, caching, and request/response transformation.

REST APIs (original) support more features: response caching, API keys, per-stage settings, request/response transforms. HTTP APIs are faster and 70% cheaper, suitable for Lambda proxy and JWT authorizers. Choose HTTP API unless you need REST-specific features.

Lambda authorizers validate a custom token (JWT, OAuth, API key) by calling a Lambda function that returns an IAM policy. Cognito authorizers validate JWTs directly without Lambda. IAM authorization signs requests with SigV4.
Apply per-API-key rate limits (requests/second) and quota limits (requests/day) with usage plans. Default throttle is 10,000 RPS per account — request a quota increase for high-traffic APIs.
Maintain persistent connections (e.g., for chat, live dashboards). API Gateway manages connection lifecycle; your Lambda or backend receives connect/disconnect/message frames via routes.
Building a Modern Data Lake on AWS

A data lake centralizes structured and unstructured data from many sources into a single scalable repository (typically S3) before it is processed or analyzed. AWS provides a complete set of services to build, govern, and query a production data lake.

Batch data arrives via AWS DataSync (from on-premises NAS/NFS), S3 Transfer Acceleration (from globally distributed clients), or AWS Database Migration Service (from relational databases). Streaming data arrives via Kinesis Data Firehose or MSK.
Storage layer — S3 as the lake:
Organize data in a three-zone pattern: raw zone (exact copy of source data, immutable), refined zone (cleaned, validated, columnar format like Parquet), and trusted zone (aggregated, business-ready).
Catalog layer — AWS Glue Data Catalog:

A central Hive-compatible metastore that maps table names to S3 locations. Athena, EMR, Redshift Spectrum, and QuickSight all use the same Glue catalog, so a table defined once is queryable from any engine.

Manages fine-grained access control on the data lake. Grant column-level and row-level permissions on Glue tables without managing S3 bucket policies. Lake Formation centralizes permissions so you control data access in one place rather than 100 different S3 policies.
Athena for ad-hoc SQL at any scale, Redshift Spectrum for joining warehouse tables with lake data, EMR for Spark transformations, and SageMaker for ML training jobs that read directly from S3.

Lake Formation enables you to grant permissions to a Glue database table with a single API call rather than updating many S3 bucket policies. This is the recommended governance pattern for new data lake deployments, especially in multi-team or multi-account organizations where data sharing is frequent.
ML in Production: Considerations Beyond Training

Training a model is only the first step. Running it reliably in production — at low latency, with data drift detection, and with model versioning — is where most ML projects stall. SageMaker provides the infrastructure to address each concern.

SageMaker Model Registry stores versioned models with metadata (training metrics, dataset lineage, approval status). A CI/CD pipeline promotes a model from Pending → Approved before it can be deployed to production endpoints.

Continuously compares the statistical distribution of production inference inputs against the training baseline. When data drift exceeds a threshold (e.g., average age of customers shifts 15%), it fires a CloudWatch alarm for retraining.
Route a copy of production traffic to a shadow variant of a new model and compare outputs without serving the new model's predictions to users. Validate quality before switching traffic.

SageMaker Endpoints support multiple production variants with weighted traffic splitting. Gradually shift traffic from the old model to the new one (e.g., 90/10 → 50/50 → 0/100) while monitoring metrics.
SageMaker Neo compiles models for specific hardware targets (ARM, x86, GPU) reducing inference latency by 2–4×. Graviton-based instances (ml.c7g) offer the best price/performance for CPU inference.
Build analytics pipelines with managed services:
Kinesis → S3 data lake → Glue ETL → Redshift or Athena is the standard AWS analytics stack. Each component is serverless or managed, requiring no cluster administration.
Use pre-built AI APIs (Rekognition, Comprehend, Textract) for common tasks with no ML expertise. Use Bedrock for LLM-powered features. Use SageMaker for custom model training and deployment.
Synchronous calls between microservices create tight coupling and cascading failures. Queues and event buses allow independent scaling, retries, and backpressure.
Orchestrate complex flows with Step Functions:
Replace Lambda-chains-calling-Lambda (tangled, untraceable) with an explicit state machine that handles retries, timeouts, parallelism, and conditional branching in one auditable place.
CDK provides type safety, IDE autocomplete, reusable construct libraries, and unit testing for infrastructure — dramatically reducing the verbosity and error rate of large CloudFormation templates.
